planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun in lean, very well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; showy stonecrop is extraordinarily drought-tolerant once established and is best planted in poor to average, dry soil — rich, moist soils cause floppy, weak growth.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and very well-drained, lean, average to poor soil; showy stonecrop thrives on neglect and drought — it rots in heavy, wet, or overly fertile soil, and rich soil produces tall, floppy stems that fall open in the center. Hylotelephium spectabile (formerly Sedum spectabile) is a succulent perennial forming neat, upright clumps of thick, fleshy, blue-green leaves on sturdy stems, producing large, flat-topped, broccoli-like flower heads that open rose-pink in August, deepen to dusty rose and brick-russet through September and October, and persist as attractive buff-brown seedheads through winter — making it one of the most important late-season perennials for pollinators, with bees, butterflies, and hoverflies crowding the flowers well into fall. The iconic cultivar 'Autumn Joy' (syn. 'Herbstfreude') is the most widely grown sedum in the world and is essentially identical to the species. Plants are best divided every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor; simply pull clumps apart and replant divisions.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun to light partial shade in well-drained, average to lean soil; orpine is extremely adaptable and long-lived, tolerating poor, dry soils and a wide range of conditions — avoid heavy, wet clay, which leads to root rot.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, average to lean, dry to moderately moist soil; orpine is one of the most tolerant and long-lived of all garden perennials — it earned the folk name 'live-forever' because detached stems and leaves can remain alive and even root themselves without soil for extended periods. Hylotelephium telephium (formerly Sedum telephium) is a Eurasian succulent perennial with thick, fleshy, toothed, blue-green to gray-green leaves and upright stems bearing flat-topped clusters of small, star-shaped, deep rose-pink to reddish-purple flowers from late summer into fall; cultivars such as 'Matrona' (with purple-flushed stems and leaves) and 'Purple Emperor' (deeply purple foliage) are highly ornamental. It has a long history as a traditional herb used in folk medicine across Europe and Asia; the leaves are edible raw or cooked. Divide every few years in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep and 3 inches apart in early to mid-fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained but moisture-retentive soil in partial to full shade; Woronow's snowdrop establishes best when planted 'in the green' immediately after flowering in late winter, but dry bulbs planted promptly in fall will succeed in suitable shade conditions.

directions: Plant bulbs 3 inches deep in early to mid-fall in moist, humus-rich, woodland soil in partial to full shade; Woronow's snowdrop is one of the most shade-tolerant snowdrops available and excels under deciduous trees and shrubs where it naturalizes freely. Galanthus woronowii is a Caucasian species snowdrop immediately distinguished from the common snowdrop (G. nivalis) by its exceptionally bright, glossy, apple-green, strap-shaped leaves that remain vivid and fresh-looking even in the coldest weather — giving rise to the common name 'Green Snowdrop'; the white, nodding, single flowers with green-marked inner petals appear from January through March, pushing through snow and frozen ground. It naturalizes readily by offsets and self-seeding under deciduous trees and performs particularly well in shaded gardens where it receives winter and spring sunlight through bare branches and summer shade; this dappled-shade tolerance makes it one of the most reliable snowdrops for woodland gardens. Like all snowdrops, it goes dormant by early summer and benefits from undisturbed, leaf-mold-enriched soil.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; alternatively, cold-stratify seeds for 60–90 days in the refrigerator and direct sow in early spring — golden alexanders requires a cold moist period to break dormancy and germinates poorly without stratification.

directions: Direct sow in fall, or cold-stratify for 60–90 days and sow in spring, in moist to average, well-drained, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; golden alexanders is one of the most shade-tolerant native wildflowers in the carrot family and is an excellent choice for lightly shaded or woodland-edge gardens with moist soil. Zizia aurea is a native North American perennial in the Apiaceae (carrot/parsley) family producing bright, golden-yellow, flat-topped umbels of tiny flowers in April through June — one of the earliest yellow-flowered natives to bloom, bridging the gap between spring bulbs and summer perennials; it is a critical early-season nectar source for native bees and an important larval host plant for the black swallowtail butterfly (Papilio polyxenes), whose caterpillars feed on the foliage. It tolerates partial shade and moist to periodically wet soils and naturalizes beautifully in rain gardens, woodland margins, and native plant meadows. Dark green, compound leaves remain attractive throughout the season after bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for natural cold stratification and spring germination; cold-stratify for 60–90 days and sow in early spring if fall sowing is not possible — like Z. aurea, heartleaf golden alexanders requires cold moist stratification to germinate reliably.

directions: Direct sow in fall, or cold-stratify for 60–90 days and sow in spring, in moist to average, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; heartleaf golden alexanders is slightly more compact than golden alexanders (Z. aurea) and equally shade-tolerant, making it a versatile native for smaller garden spaces, shaded borders, and rain gardens. Zizia aptera is distinguished from Z. aurea primarily by its simple, heart-shaped (cordate) basal leaves — unlike the pinnately compound lower leaves of Z. aurea — though both species bear bright golden-yellow umbels of tiny flowers in April through June and are nearly identical in garden performance and ecological function; both serve as host plants for the black swallowtail butterfly and provide early-season pollen and nectar for native bees, including specialist carrot-family bees. Heartleaf golden alexanders tolerates a wider range of soil conditions, including drier soils, than Z. aurea, and is somewhat more adaptable in garden settings. It naturalizes readily and combines beautifully with ferns, native sedges, wild ginger, and other shade-tolerant natives.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring around or just before the last frost date in lean, well-drained, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; sundrops self-seeds readily once established and spreads by rhizomes to form attractive colonies — site it where it has room to naturalize.

directions: Direct sow in early spring in lean, well-drained, average to poor, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; sundrops is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in poor, dry soils — rich, moist conditions produce weak, floppy growth and reduce flowering. Oenothera fruticosa is a native eastern North American perennial evening primrose relative that, unlike most of its nocturnal relatives, opens its bright, butter-yellow, 4-petaled, 1–2-inch flowers in full daylight throughout the day — making it a true daytime ornamental rather than a night-bloomer; the clear yellow flowers appear abundantly from May through July atop reddish, upright stems with lance-shaped, glossy, dark-green leaves that often develop attractive red and bronze tints in fall and spring. It is an excellent native wildflower for sunny, dry, low-maintenance borders and meadow plantings, spreads gradually by rhizomes and self-seeding to form colonies, and is highly attractive to bees and other native pollinators. The red-budded subspecies glauca (sometimes sold as ssp. glauca or 'Fyrverkeri'/'Fireworks') has particularly ornamental red flower buds that contrast vividly with the yellow open flowers.
